ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Mar-10-2016 Set For Case Management Conference On Jun-08-16 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MANDATORY SETTLEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-08-2016 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Feb-26-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. STANLEY C. GOFF JR., ESQ. Tel No. 415-571-9570 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. CHANTEL E. LAFRADES, ESQ. of Murchison & Cumming LLP, Tel No. 415-524-4300 appearing for Defendant Commus LLC dba Food Express.

Matter argued and submitted by counsel. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.
Defendant Food Express' motion for summary judgment is granted. Defendant sustained its burden of production and plaintiff did not present any evidence creating a triable issue on the third, fourth, fifth and sixth causes of action in plaintiff's amended complaint and plaintiff concedes that defendant is entitled to judgment on the seventh cause of action. As to the third through sixth claims, the undisputed facts establish that defendant is not liable for the misconduct of its employee under the principles of respondent superior because the employee's conduct was outside the scope of employment. (Mary M. v. City of Los Angeles (1991) 54 Cal.3d 202, 208-209.) It is undisputed that the employee is a restaurant worker who washes dishes and does not perform any security functions such as dealing with unruly customers. (UMF # 18-20.) There is nothing in the nature of the employee's duties that predictably created a risk that the employee would batter, falsely arrest, or falsely imprison a customer. The fact that defendant did not discipline the employee after the incident is evidence that there may have been ratification and whether an employee has ratified an employee's conduct is generally a factual question. (Baptist v. Robinson (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 151, 169-170.) (Response to UMF # 21.) However, ratification requires that 1) the employee intended to act on behalf of the employer, 2) the employer learned of the conduct after it occurred, and 3) the employer adopted the conduct. (CACI 3710.) The undisputed facts establish that the employee did not intend to act on behalf of defendant. Plaintiff does not dispute that the employee's conduct had nothing to do with the employee's employment or the restaurant and the dispute concerned a personal argument between the plaintiff and the employee regarding the employee's belief that plaintiff stole money from her as a part of a Chinese ghost scam. (UMF # 3- 6.) Plaintiff also acknowledges that the employee's conduct was of no benefit to defendant. established that Plaintiff cannot present evidence that Defendant knew or should have known about the employee's unfitness. (UMF # 23-26.) Plaintiff's request for a continuance is denied. A continuance is warranted where the discovery sought is material and necessary and the discovery could not have been obtained earlier. (See Combs v. Skyriver Communications Inc. (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1242, 1270.) The discovery that plaintiff seeks is unnecessary because it is undisputed that defendant did not discipline the employee and the undisputed facts state that the incident occurred in the restaurant. (UMF # 2, 7-13.) ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 (302/HK)

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Sep-29-2015 Continued To Mar-28-16 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-13-2015 Set For Trial On Jan-04-16 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-07-2015 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. KATELYN M. KNIGHT of Murchison & Cumming LLP, Tel No. (415) 524-4300 appearing for Defendant Commus LLC dba Food Express;
No other appearances made in this matter.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT COMMUS LLC DBA FOOD EXPRESS' DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT is granted in part and overruled in part. The Demurrer is granted without leave to amend as to the first and second causes of action. The one-year statute of limitations for libel and slander (Code Civ. Proc., 335, 340, subd. 3) is applicable to claims for invasion of privacy. [See Weiner v. Superior Court, (1976) 58 Cal. App. 3d 525.] The Demurrer is overruled as to the fourth cause of action. The FAC adequately sets forth the ultimate facts necessary to establish each element of the cause of action. (See FAC at 25-27.) ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Melanie Dawn Gheno, CSR #7489 (302/EHG)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jan-26-2015 Continued To May-27-15 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610
